name: temple-of-craft
summary: A temple where worship and making are the same act — a forge-shrine, a scriptorium-chapel, a loom-sanctuary. The tools of the craft are the altar furniture; the craft itself is the rite.

size_guidance.aspect_ratio: rectangular — altar at one end, working space at the other

accent.purpose: the altar/focus floor — typically more sacred material

primary_palette.note: smith-god temple — the forge is the altar, an anvil as chancel furniture
weaver-god temple — the loom is the altar, threads dedicated as offerings
scribe-god temple — the altar holds the sacred text; desks and scrolls around
a working forge-shrine; less altar-focused, more hearth-centered

story_prompts: a consecration-work in progress — a priest-artisan mid-craft, the work itself the rite
between rites — tools laid reverently in their racks, the hearth banked
a workshop stripped of its gods — the forge cold, the ward-stones carried off, sigils chiseled away
a masterwork in progress — the altar bears an item nearly finished, a gift for a great patron
a craftmaster's funeral — the anvil draped, the forge lit and the forge-altar burning bright

lighting_notes.ambient_intent: warm and working (0.45-0.55) — forge-glow dominant, with candles and wall-lanterns at the altar end

anti_patterns: the craft is the rite — the working tools are holy, not ornamental clutter
avoid pews — craftspeople stand at benches; this is not a congregant space
the altar and the workshop share the room — never split them into separate chambers
